nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving streams and tributaries with driftwood, submerged branches, and leaf litter; strongly associated with woody debris and reed beds where its twig-like shape provides camouflage

decorPreferences: driftwood and submerged branches (essential — resting and grazing surface)
dense planting to provide cover and additional grazing surfaces

diet.notes: True aufwuchs grazer — rasps biofilm, algae, and microorganisms from wood and rock surfaces continuously throughout the day. Requires a mature, well-established tank with a healthy algae/biofilm bloom. Supplement with algae wafers, spirulina wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach), and sinking herbivore pellets. Will also graze soft driftwood surfaces. Protein-rich foods should be minimal; a diet too heavy in protein can cause digestive issues. Will decline rapidly in sterile or newly set-up tanks lacking sufficient biofilm — treat similarly to Otocinclus in this regard.

goodWith: small plecos that occupy different grazing zones
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish that cause stress
fish competing directly for algae grazing zones in small tanks
high-flow setups with inadequate calm areas

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Farlowella acus showing characteristic elongated twig-like body shape.

summary: A slender, twig-mimicking loricariid catfish from Venezuela and the Orinoco basin, reaching up to 20 cm. An obligate aufwuchs grazer requiring a mature, algae-rich tank. Peaceful and reclusive; intermediate care due to its sensitivity to algae shortage and poor water quality. Trade specimens labelled “Farlowella acus” often represent multiple species in the genus.
careNotes:
The Farlowella catfish is one of the most distinctive-looking loricariids in the hobby, with a hyper-elongated rostrum and body that closely mimics a drifting twig or plant stem — a camouflage strategy that makes it nearly invisible among submerged wood and leaf litter. Specimens sold in the trade as “Farlowella acus” frequently represent other species within the Farlowella genus complex, as reliable species-level identification requires specialist morphological assessment; for aquarium purposes all are kept identically.

The single most critical husbandry requirement is a mature tank with well-established biofilm and algal growth on driftwood and hardscape. Farlowella are constant, low-intensity grazers that spend most of their day slowly moving across surfaces rasping aufwuchs. In a tank lacking sufficient biofilm — especially newly set up tanks — they will slowly starve even when algae wafers and vegetables are offered, as they are poorly adapted to picking up food from the substrate. A tank of at least six months’ maturity is strongly recommended before introducing this species; treat it as you would Otocinclus in terms of tank-age dependence.

Water quality must be pristine with low nitrates (under 20 ppm ideally) and stable, soft, slightly acidic to neutral conditions. Avoid hard or alkaline water. Moderate to low flow suits them best; they are not strong swimmers and high turbulence can be stressful. Temperature should remain stable — sudden drops are poorly tolerated.

Farlowella are shy and largely nocturnal in activity, spending daytime hours clinging motionlessly to driftwood or plant stems. They should not be kept with boisterous or fast-moving fish. The species is a poor competitor at feeding time; ensure algae wafers and blanched vegetables are placed directly near their resting areas. Driftwood is essentially mandatory — it provides both a resting substrate and a crucial supplementary food source as they graze its surface biofilm and ingested cellulose aids digestion.

[Merge consolidation 2026-06-10]: Consolidated with retired entry fw-fish-539 (Twig Catfish / Farlowella spp.). Trade specimens of "Twig Catfish" / "Farlowella" cover a genus complex (Farlowella spp.); F. acus is the most-cited canonical species.
breedingNotes: Farlowella breed in captivity with moderate frequency when conditions are right. The male guards and fans a clutch of eggs deposited on a flat, clean surface — often the aquarium glass, a broad leaf, or smooth slate. Eggs hatch in approximately 7–10 days. The male provides all parental care until the fry are free-swimming. Fry resemble miniature adults and require the same biofilm-rich diet; microwaved zucchini slices placed near them are commonly recommended as a first food alongside natural biofilm growth. Breeding is more reliably triggered in soft, slightly acidic, well-oxygenated water. Separating the breeding pair into a species tank significantly improves success rates.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Farlowella acus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, water parameters (temperature 23–27°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm/~2–12 dGH), native range (Venezuela, Colombia, Trinidad), habitat (slow streams with woody debris), diet (aufwuchs/biofilm), tank requirements, breeding behaviour, and conspecific/interspecific compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Farlowella acus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 20.6 cm TL), native range (Orinoco basin, Venezuela), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), and diet classification (herbivore/aufwuchs). Confirmed temperature range 23–27°C.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Twig Catfish (Farlowella)
additional.notes: Trade-focused care guide confirming mature-tank requirement analogous to Otocinclus, aufwuchs grazing behaviour, driftwood necessity, soft slightly acidic water preference, peaceful temperament, and breeding on flat surfaces with paternal egg guarding.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Whiptail and Twig Catfishes
additional.notes: Provided context on the Farlowella genus complex and the frequent misidentification of trade specimens; confirmed ~20 cm maximum size, Venezuela and Orinoco drainage origin, camouflage strategy as twig/stick mimic, intermediate care level due to water quality and algae demands.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Loricariid Catfishes of the Orinoco
additional.notes: Background on Loricariidae diversity and Farlowella natural habitat in Venezuelan Orinoco tributaries; confirmed association with submerged woody debris, slow currents, and biofilm-rich aufwuchs feeding strategy.

fish.breedingNotes: Males guard and fan egg clutches deposited on flat surfaces (glass, slate, broad leaves). Eggs hatch in 7–10 days; male provides sole parental care until fry are free-swimming. Fry require abundant biofilm; blanched zucchini is a recommended early food. Species tank significantly improves success.
